Jeff: Okay, this is Nicholas Wilton's PLC2 for his Art2Life Program. I'm just going to
play the beginning of this. Again, the audio's not going to be awesome. We're
giving you the entire video to watch so my apologies for the audio and this screen
capture not being great.
Nicholas: Welcome back. I'm super excited for this video, number two, where we're going to
be delving into value, the second of the six Art/Life principles. First of all, I just
want to say thank you for all your comments and questions. It's really helpful, it's
helpful for everybody. It's also really helpful for me because I can see where I'm
missing things and where I can clarify more.
Jeff: A couple little things. Again, I really like Nicholas' presence on camera. It's just so
natural and unassuming. He's not super slick or super professional but you don't
need to be super slick or super professional.
I don't like the very first one or two seconds. It looks ... where he's scratching his
back, he's not looking at the camera so I would have liked a little tighter cut there
maybe. They didn't need that first one or two seconds. Again, I think this just
works. It just works.
Okay. Let's go to the script. This is the script or more accurately, this is the
transcript. Again, I don't know if he delivered this from a script or if he scripted
this out. My best guess is he didn't. Whenever you look at transcripts, sometimes
things read awkwardly. When we're speaking, we don't speak the way we write so
take that under advisement so as you read through this, some of it might seem
awkward in places but when he delivers this, it's not awkward.
Remember, Nicholas did $400,000 with this launch. Basically starting from
scratch, teaching people how to make art.
Extraordinary launch. Extraordinary results.
He starts off and, this is his introduction, I guess this whole piece here [shown
in video] is his introduction. Basically, I would have liked him to do a little bit
more with this. He just says: "I'm super excited about this video. We're going to
be delving into value, the second of the Art/Life principles." That's basically his
introduction then here, he's responding to the response, responding to the
comments and this is great, it's fantastic:

It built social proof. “Thanks for all the comments and questions." Social proof
is like, ‘Wow, if someone's watching his videos, so then he must be worth
watching.’ It's also where he says: "It's really helpful and helpful for everyone. It's
helpful for me because I can see where I'm missing things and where I can clarify
more." There, he's showing some real humility there, that he doesn't know
everything. "It's helpful for me to see what I'm missing." That makes you more
likable. I don't think Nicholas said, "I'm going to drop this in here for social proof
and to try to be likable," that's just who he is but I'm just showing you what it
does within the context of the script.
Here he recaps the last video: "We talked about design," and really, his recap
is really this part right here: "In the last video, we talked about design." Here he's
recapping his idea for design. This is his whole intro right here. It's his whole
intro. Note over here, I have: "There is no recap of the problem that he's solving,
no recap of the pain." If you'll recap back, PLC1, it was like, one day you'll turn up
and you're creating great art, the next day you'll turn up and you're creating
horrible art. You never know what's going to happen when you show up in the
studio. He went into that in the first video but in this video, he didn't recap that at
all. He didn't recap what his solution is or how he found that solution, he didn't
recap who he was, what authority he has, what his positioning is so if someone
did not see PLC1, this is just some guy standing in front of a board and drawing
stuff. I think he would have been really well served if he'd put in a minute
here of recapping the pain, the problem, the solution and who he is.
But again, $400,000 launch, just an example. You don't have to be perfect.
Then, he jumps in by the one minute mark, he's already teaching and he goes
deep in this video with the teaching. He's still teaching down here. He's still
teaching down here. Here, this is good because he's still teaching, he's in full on
teaching mode. Total full on teaching mode.
We're about eight and a half to nine minutes in, full on teaching mode but he
brings it back to his principles. “Now, what I love about getting these principles,
getting this in your head is you don't really need to know where I'm going and
where my outcome is.” You just know these principles and things are going to
work out. That's great, it's establishing the value of the principles.
Now, how cool would it be if up here, he talked about ... in his intro, he talked
about how he has struggled and he came up with all these principles and now I'm
going to teach you the principles? It would make this piece more powerful. I
mean, as it is, it's plenty powerful but it would be even more powerful because

right now, if they're seeing the word principles, they have to remember back to
the first video where he talked about principles. Okay, moving along.
Here, he does a good job of building and it's still teaching. Still teaching. Full out
teaching. Does a good job building the value of his approach.
“Once you start getting this, it becomes second nature and you're just kind of, ‘I
think I'm going to make this bigger, it's going to look better.’ You can't help it.
You can't turn this off.” I really love that again, he's building the value of his
approach and building the values of his principles. It's really cool.
Here, he goes back to the pain and back to the potential solution here. Again, it
would be way more powerful if he had put it in his introduction but at least he's
got it here. "I want to show you what can happen. What happens a lot is that we
lose track of the value and design but there's so much happening. We got color,
we're trying to make something look like something, there's textures, there's
parts we fall in love with. What I'm telling you is, you get this right …” and here,
he's basically saying this: “This idea of design and value and loud conversation,
these are so strong that you can't compete with them. If they're not working, your
work is just ... well, you can't fight it. It's exhausting. It takes so long. Usually
something happens but it happens by accident and you start fixing it. If you go
back and look at your best work that people love, that you love, then I guarantee
you that these things are working in it." I think if he would have said, "These
principles are working in it," then it would have been better but at least he's
getting back to the pain and the idea of a potential solution.
Now he's back, still in teaching mode. We're 19 minutes in. Now here's a call to
action that he just sort of drops in. Okay, there's no transition into this other
than, "Okay, ask questions about this or put up a picture of your own and we can
break it apart again. It's choosing an area of your work where you need to let go."
This is a call to action and it's a good call to action. He actually asked them to
put up a picture of their own, to post it. I like that. Would like a little more
transition here and then down here, he's building the value of his solution. “Do
you see how you've got all the tools you need to empower your work, to really
create impact? You get to decide what you want to say. It's allowing you to
communicate your ideas more carefully, make it more attractive and more
seductive so someone takes notice. That's what I love about it.” He's building the
value of his solution here.
